phrases.cultural_note: The crypt houses Victor Hugo, Marie Curie, Alexandre Dumas, Voltaire, Rousseau, Émile Zola, and other 'great men and women' of France.

phrases.text_local: Où est le pendule de Foucault ?
phrases.pronunciation: oo eh luh pahn-DOOL duh foo-KOH
phrases.cultural_note: Foucault demonstrated Earth's rotation here in 1851 with his famous pendulum. A replica still swings from the dome — watch the sand trail shift over time.

phrases.intent_en: Ask about access to the dome
phrases.text_local: Peut-on visiter la colonnade en haut ?
phrases.pronunciation: puh-TOHN vee-zee-TAY lah koh-loh-NAD ahn OH
phrases.cultural_note: The colonnade gallery at the top is open seasonally (April to October). It offers a stunning view of the Latin Quarter and all of Paris.
The crypt is included in the admission ticket. The tombs are simple and dignified. Marie Curie's tomb is lined with lead due to her radioactive research.

phrases.intent_en: Ask what this building was originally
phrases.text_local: C'était quoi à l'origine, ce bâtiment ?
phrases.pronunciation: seh-TEH kwah ah loh-ree-ZHEEN, suh bah-tee-MAHN
phrases.cultural_note: Originally built as a church dedicated to Saint Geneviève, it was converted during the Revolution to honor France's greatest citizens.
